_meta.what: Authored knowledge layer for the 13-scene platform: CI tool alternatives, signers, SBOM tools, registries, frontend observability, SRE pack, scene notes. graph.json stays the extracted truth; this file is the curated truth.

ciAlternatives.what: Static analysis — finds vulnerable code patterns without running the app

options.name: CodeQL
options.pick: GitHub-hosted code — deepest dataflow analysis, zero infra
options.tradeoff: vs Semgrep: deeper taint tracking, slower scans, GitHub-bound

options.name: Semgrep
options.pick: Fast custom rules, any CI, monorepos
options.avoid: You need cross-function taint flow on the free tier
options.tradeoff: vs CodeQL: minutes faster, rules are simpler but shallower

options.name: SonarQube
options.pick: Quality gates + security in one dashboard, self-hosted mandates
options.avoid: You only want security findings — heavier to run
options.tradeoff: vs both: adds maintainability metrics, needs its own server

ciAlternatives.what: Dependency scanning — known CVEs in the libraries you import

options.name: Dependabot
options.pick: Default on GitHub — alerts + auto-PRs with zero setup
options.tradeoff: vs Renovate: simpler, far less configurable

options.name: Renovate
options.pick: Grouped updates, schedules, monorepos, any forge
options.tradeoff: vs Dependabot: more power, more config to own

options.name: Trivy
options.pick: One scanner for deps + images + IaC in CI
options.avoid: You need auto-fix PRs (it only reports)
options.tradeoff: vs Snyk: free and local, no fix suggestions

options.name: Snyk
options.pick: Fix PRs + reachability analysis + dev-friendly UI
options.tradeoff: vs Trivy: better fixes, costs money, cloud-dependent

ciAlternatives.what: Secret scanning — credentials committed into the repo

options.name: Gitleaks
options.pick: Fast regex+entropy scan in pre-commit AND CI
options.avoid: You need verified-secret checks (it flags patterns only)
options.tradeoff: vs TruffleHog: lighter, no live credential verification

options.name: TruffleHog
options.pick: Verifies if found secrets are LIVE against providers
options.tradeoff: vs Gitleaks: fewer false positives, slower, heavier

options.name: GitHub Push Protection
options.pick: Blocks the push itself — secrets never land
options.tradeoff: vs both: earliest gate, GitHub-only, fixed patterns

options.name: Checkov
options.pick: Widest coverage: Terraform, K8s, Docker, Helm, ARM
options.tradeoff: vs tfsec: broader targets, noisier defaults

options.name: Trivy (config)
options.pick: Already using Trivy — one tool, one report format
options.tradeoff: vs Checkov: fewer built-in policies, simpler ops

options.name: KICS
options.tradeoff: vs Checkov: comparable coverage, less community momentum

ciAlternatives.what: License compliance — what licenses your dependencies carry

options.name: license-checker / pip-licenses
options.avoid: You need org-wide policy + legal workflows
options.tradeoff: vs FOSSA: free but per-language and report-only

options.name: FOSSA
options.pick: Org policy engine, legal reporting, attribution docs
options.avoid: Single small service on a budget
options.tradeoff: vs CLIs: complete governance, costs real money

ciAlternatives.what: Dynamic testing — attacks the RUNNING app like an outsider

options.name: OWASP ZAP
options.pick: Free baseline scan of a deployed preview env
options.avoid: No running environment exists in CI
options.tradeoff: vs Nuclei: full proxy/spider, slower, heavier

options.name: Nuclei
options.pick: Fast template-based checks on known CVE patterns

signers.name: cosign (Sigstore)
signers.pick: Keyless signing via CI OIDC — no key management at all
signers.avoid: Policy mandates long-lived org keys outside Sigstore
signers.tradeoff: vs Notation: zero key ops, depends on Sigstore infra

signers.name: Notation (CNCF)
signers.pick: Enterprise PKI — sign with your own certificate chain
signers.avoid: You don't run a PKI and don't want to
signers.tradeoff: vs cosign: your keys your control, you own rotation

sbomTools.name: syft
sbomTools.pick: Default: fast, accurate, pairs with cosign attest

sbomTools.name: trivy sbom
sbomTools.pick: Already running Trivy — one tool fewer

registries.name: GHCR
registries.pick: GitHub Actions default — GITHUB_TOKEN auth, zero stored secrets
registries.setup: No OIDC setup needed; enable branch protection + secret scanning

registries.name: Amazon ECR
registries.pick: Deploying into AWS/EKS — same-cloud pulls, IAM-native
registries.setup: Create repo with scanOnPush; add OIDC provider + IAM role for the repo
registries.login: aws ecr get-login-password --region $AWS_REGION | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin $AWS_ACCOUNT.dkr.ecr.$AWS_REGION.amazonaws.com

registries.name: Google Artifact Registry
registries.setup: gcloud artifacts repositories create + Workload Identity Federation for GitHub

registries.name: Azure ACR
registries.setup: az acr create + federated credential for the GitHub repo

registries.name: Harbor
registries.setup: Deploy Harbor (Helm), create robot account, store its token as a CI secret
registries.residency: Wherever YOU run it — full control

registries.name: Quay.io
registries.pick: Red Hat ecosystem, built-in Clair scanning
registries.setup: Create robot account; token as CI secret

registries.name: Docker Hub
registries.pick: Public OSS images with the widest reach
registries.avoid: Private enterprise images (rate limits, weaker controls)

registries.name: JFrog Artifactory
registries.pick: One registry for containers + npm + maven + generic artifacts
registries.setup: Repo + access token; optional OIDC on paid tiers

registries.name: Nexus Repository
registries.pick: Existing Nexus shop — keep one artifact store
registries.setup: Docker-hosted repo + user token as CI secret

frontendObservability.name: Sentry
frontendObservability.what: Error tracking + session replay for the browser
frontendObservability.pick: Default for FE errors — source-mapped stack traces, releases
frontendObservability.avoid: Strict data-residency without self-hosting (or use their EU/self-host)
frontendObservability.tradeoff: vs Faro: richer errors/replay, separate backend from your metrics

frontendObservability.name: web-vitals
frontendObservability.what: Google's library for LCP/INP/CLS — real-user performance
frontendObservability.pick: Always — 2KB, feeds Core Web Vitals to any backend
frontendObservability.tradeoff: Numbers only; pair with an error tracker

frontendObservability.name: OpenTelemetry Web
frontendObservability.what: OTel SDK for the browser — traces from click to backend
frontendObservability.pick: You already run OTel server-side and want ONE pipeline end-to-end
frontendObservability.avoid: Quick setups — browser OTel is still maturing
frontendObservability.tradeoff: vs Sentry: unified vendor-neutral traces, weaker error UX

frontendObservability.name: Grafana Faro
frontendObservability.what: Grafana's FE SDK — logs, errors, vitals into Loki/Tempo
frontendObservability.pick: Grafana LGTM stack shops — FE lands in the same dashboards
frontendObservability.tradeoff: vs Sentry: one stack, younger product

frontendObservability.name: Datadog RUM
frontendObservability.pick: Already paying Datadog — FE joins your APM traces

sre.sloStarter: Availability SLO 99.9% monthly (43m error budget). Burn-rate alerts: page at 14.4x/1h, ticket at 6x/6h. Why 99.9: one nine cheaper than your dependencies' composite.
sre.retentionNote: Log/audit retention is regime-driven — see the rulebook: PCI 1yr (3m hot), HIPAA 6yr, OSFI/B-13 7yr, SOC 2 1yr typical-evidence window defined in your policy.

sceneNotes.1: The industry choice changes the RULES, never the steps. Every industry walks the same 18 scenes; the lens only marks which rules bite.
sceneNotes.2: No industry forces a framework — choose freely. It only sets your language, which then decides package manager, ORM and base image.
sceneNotes.3: Scaffold + git rules are the SAME for every industry — branch protection, CODEOWNERS, signed commits. Regulated industries only ADD reviewers/evidence on top.
sceneNotes.4: Auth, data layer and observability are the SAME code patterns for every industry. What changes: whether a regime FORCES auth/audit on — not how you write it.
sceneNotes.5: The local loop is the SAME for every industry. Pre-commit hooks are universal hygiene; no regime changes them.
sceneNotes.6: The Dockerfile is structurally identical everywhere — multi-stage, non-root, minimal base. Regulated industries only swap the base (FIPS) and add scan gates.
sceneNotes.7: The PR gate runs the same checks for every industry. Regulated ones raise thresholds and add reviewers — they never remove a gate.
sceneNotes.8: The build → sign → SBOM → push flow is identical everywhere. Compliance changes WHICH registry/runtime, never the supply-chain steps.
sceneNotes.9: Helm/Kustomize structure is the SAME for every industry. What changes per industry is the VALUES (replicas, regions, network policy) — not the mechanism.
sceneNotes.10: Argo CD hub-and-spoke is the same for every industry. What changes: which cluster (data residency) and the network/policy values — not the mechanism.
sceneNotes.11: The four golden signals are universal. Regimes change RETENTION and whether alerts page a human — Scene 1's rulebook decides that.
sceneNotes.12: Integrations are the one mostly industry-SPECIFIC scene — a bank talks to payment rails, a hospital to an EHR. The gateway + auth pattern in front is the same.
sceneNotes.13: The audit is the same shape for every industry — every Scene-1 rule traced to where it's met. Only the LIST of rules differs.
sceneNotes.14: The weekly update loop is identical everywhere — a bot opens PRs, you merge while small. Some regimes require a logged approval for majors.
sceneNotes.15: Platform upgrades follow the same staging-first path everywhere. Regulated ones add a change-control record; the technical steps don't change.
sceneNotes.16: Rotate-backup-drill is universal hygiene. Regimes set the FREQUENCY and evidence retention — not the mechanism.
sceneNotes.17: The incident path — page, mitigate, learn — is the same everywhere. Regulated industries add a regulator-notification clock; the runbook is otherwise identical.
sceneNotes.18: The quarterly EOL / cost / sunset review is the same for every industry. Retention and archival rules at retirement come from Scene 1's rulebook.

glossary.def: A machine-readable inventory of every library, OS package, and dependency inside a container image. Like an ingredients list for software.

glossary.full: Supply chain Levels for Software Artifacts
glossary.def: A security framework defining integrity levels for the build process. Level 3 requires an isolated build job with signed provenance. Pronounced "salsa".
A scanner that attacks your running application like an external attacker. Sends malicious HTTP inputs and checks for vulnerable responses. OWASP ZAP is the most common open-source DAST tool.
A scanner that reads source code without running it to find security bug patterns. Semgrep and CodeQL are examples. Catches injection, hardcoded secrets, OWASP Top 10.
Scans package manifests (package.json, go.mod, requirements.txt) for known CVEs in open-source dependencies. The "dependency audit" category of security scanning.
Scans Dockerfile, Kubernetes YAML, and Terraform files for security misconfigurations. Checkov is the most common open-source tool. Maps findings to CIS benchmarks.
A public database entry for a known security vulnerability. Format: CVE-YEAR-NUMBER. Severity rated by CVSS score. CRITICAL = 9.0+, HIGH = 7.0–8.9, MEDIUM = 4.0–6.9.
A 0–10 score for vulnerability severity. CRITICAL ≥9.0. HIGH 7.0–8.9. MEDIUM 4.0–6.9. LOW 0.1–3.9. Informational = 0.
A minimal container base image containing only the application runtime — no shell, no package manager, no OS tools. Created by Google. Dramatically smaller attack surface than Alpine or Debian.
Signing without a stored private key. The signing key is derived from a short-lived OIDC identity token. The key exists only for the duration of the signing operation — nothing persists.
An open-source project providing free infrastructure for signing software artifacts. Components: cosign (signing tool), Rekor (transparency log), Fulcio (certificate authority for OIDC-based certs).
A public, append-only transparency log for software signing events. Every cosign signing operation creates an immutable entry. Anyone can verify when and how an artifact was signed.
An authentication protocol that lets a service (like a CI runner) prove its identity without a stored password. The CI provider issues a short-lived signed token that the registry IAM policy trusts.
A JSON standard for reporting security scan results. GitHub Security tab reads SARIF files natively. Every scanner in this pipeline outputs SARIF format.
An ISO standard for describing software packages and their licenses. SPDX-JSON is the machine-readable format used for SBOMs in this pipeline. Each package gets an SPDX license identifier.
A standard identifier for software packages across all ecosystems. Format: pkg:TYPE/NAMESPACE/NAME@VERSION. Example: pkg:npm/%40angular/core@17.0.0. Used in SBOMs.
A vulnerability database for open-source software, maintained by Google. More comprehensive for open-source than NVD alone. Used by Trivy and dependency-review-action.

A Dockerfile pattern with multiple FROM stages. The builder stage compiles the code. The runtime stage copies only the compiled output — discarding all build tools. Result: smaller, more secure final image.
A framework for managing and running git commit hooks. The same hooks run locally (before each commit) and in CI (to catch --no-verify bypasses). Hook list stored in .pre-commit-config.yaml.
A Kubernetes mechanism that intercepts API requests before they are persisted. Kyverno and OPA Gatekeeper act as admission controllers to enforce policies at pod deploy time.
A Kubernetes policy engine that enforces rules at pod admission. Used in this pipeline to verify cosign signatures before any pod starts. Configured as fail-closed (I-20).

A deployment pattern where new code receives a small % of traffic (e.g., 5%) while the rest stays on old code. Traffic shifts to 100% if error rate stays low. Rolls back automatically if error rate spikes.
The 10 most critical web application security risks, published annually by OWASP. Includes injection, broken access control, XSS, cryptographic failures. SAST tools check code for these patterns.
A fast open-source SAST tool that matches code patterns using rule files written in YAML. 1200+ security rules available. Used in S3 for OWASP Top-10 scanning across all language stacks.
An open-source vulnerability scanner by Aqua Security. Scans container images, SBOMs, and filesystems for CVEs. Supports SARIF output. Default tool for container scanning in this pipeline.
An open-source IaC scanner by Bridgecrew. Checks Dockerfile, K8s YAML, Terraform against 2000+ security rules. Maps every finding to a CIS benchmark or CVE identifier.
An open-source secret scanner. Detects API keys, passwords, private keys in git history and staged files using regex patterns for 150+ secret types. Runs in pre-commit and in CI.
A commercial license compliance tool. Scans dependency trees to detect open-source licenses conflicting with commercial use. Used in S3b. Blocks GPL/AGPL in commercial projects.
An open-source SBOM generator by Anchore. Reads container images and filesystems, identifies every package, and produces SBOMs in SPDX-JSON or CycloneDX format. Used in S8a.
An open-source load testing tool written in Go. Test scripts are written in JavaScript. Measures p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput (req/s), and error rate under simulated concurrent load.

An open-source runtime security tool. Uses eBPF to monitor syscalls inside running containers. Alerts when containers do unexpected things: spawn shells, write outside expected paths, make unusual network connections.
A Linux kernel technology that runs sandboxed programs in the kernel for observability and security. Falco uses it to monitor container syscalls without modifying the kernel or containers.
A code coverage reporting service. Tracks test coverage percentage over time and comments on PRs when coverage decreases. Enforces Invariant I-9.
A GitHub service that automatically creates PRs to update outdated dependencies. Paired with branch protection (I-15), it keeps CVE counts low without manual effort.
An open-source alternative to Dependabot. Supports more package managers and more configuration options. Creates automatic update PRs for dependencies and GitHub Actions versions.

All HTML is built in the browser using JavaScript. The server returns an empty shell HTML + JS bundle. Frameworks: React SPA, Vue + Vite, Angular. Runtime image: nginx (static file server, no Node.js). Port: 80.

HTML is built on the server on every request. The server runs JavaScript to produce HTML before sending to the browser. Frameworks: Remix, SvelteKit. Runtime image: Node.js or distroless/nodejs. Port: 3000+.
HTML is built at CI build time, not at request time. The server returns pre-built HTML files. Frameworks: Gatsby, Astro (default). Runtime image: nginx. Port: 80. No server attack surface at runtime.

Next.js feature. Pages are pre-built at build time (SSG), then re-generated in the background after a revalidation period. Combines SSG speed with near-real-time content updates. Requires a running Node.js server.

A cryptographic statement attached to a container image in the registry. Created by cosign. Contains machine-readable data (like an SBOM or SLSA provenance) plus a signature that proves the CI system produced it. Stored as OCI artifact (.att suffix).

The standard file format for SLSA provenance attestations. File extension: .intoto.jsonl. Contains a signed envelope with: builder identity, source commit, build steps, and output digest. Verifiable with slsa-verifier.

A container image that contains binaries for multiple CPU architectures (linux/amd64 + linux/arm64). Built with docker buildx --platform. Required when deploying to ARM nodes (AWS Graviton, Apple Silicon dev machines).

Docker CLI plugin that extends docker build with multi-platform support, layer cache export, and BuildKit features. Required for linux/amd64 + linux/arm64 cross-compilation in CI. Enabled by docker/setup-buildx-action@v3.

A processor emulator used in multi-arch builds to run ARM binaries on amd64 CI runners. Required when cross-compiling languages that do not support native cross-compilation (non-Go). Installed by docker/setup-qemu-action@v3.
A tree representation of source code structure used by SAST tools. Semgrep parses code into ASTs to apply pattern-matching security rules without executing the code.
A Git checkout option that fetches the full commit history instead of only the latest commit. Required by Gitleaks to scan all historical commits for leaked secrets.
A Kubernetes controller that extends Deployments with canary, blue-green, and analysis-based promotion. Used in Phase 4 for progressive delivery with automatic error-rate rollback.
A GitOps controller that pulls desired state from Git and applies it to the cluster — same pull model as Argo CD, but without Argo's built-in UI.
A sealed box holding your app and everything it needs, so it runs identically on any computer. Docker is the tool that builds and runs them.
The warehouse where container images are stored and versioned. CI pushes images in; clusters pull images out. GHCR, ECR and Harbor are registries.
A group of machines that run your containers together, managed by Kubernetes. It restarts crashed apps and spreads load automatically.
Robots that build and test every code change automatically, before it can be merged. GitHub Actions is the CI used here.
A library that lets your code talk to the database using normal objects instead of hand-written SQL. Prisma and GORM are ORMs.
A second proof beyond the password — a phone prompt, code, or security key. Stops stolen-password logins.
A US/Canada government standard for validated encryption modules. Regulated industries must run crypto that passed this validation.
The exact, frozen list of every dependency version your project uses. It makes every install identical — on your laptop and in CI.
Code that runs on every request before your logic — checking sign-in, writing audit logs, counting metrics.
Attaching JavaScript to server-rendered HTML so the page becomes interactive.
An older Angular helper that watches for changes to refresh the screen.
A token claim stating how strongly the user's identity was verified.
A token claim listing how the user proved identity (password, MFA).
The public keys a server publishes so others can verify its tokens.
A check that stops a stolen login code from being reused.
A hardened OAuth profile built for banking-level security.
A standard giving each service a verifiable identity in a mesh.
A signed piece of data you can verify was not changed.
A way to return OAuth responses signed, so they can't be tampered with.
Sending login parameters server-to-server first, so they can't be altered.
Apps registering themselves with the login server automatically.
Binds a token to one app, so a stolen token is useless.
Angular's modern, fine-grained way to track and update changing values.

1.plain: Every product is built FOR someone. Pick who — a bank, a hospital, a store. That choice writes the rulebook every later step must obey.
1.youHave: the rulebook — the rules your service must follow
1.next: pick the tool you'll build with

2.plain: A framework is a ready-made foundation so you don't build from zero. Pick the one that fits what you're making.
2.youHave: your foundation chosen, with its strengths and limits known

3.plain: Scaffolding creates your project's folder and files in one command. The repo (your project's shared home with history) is born here, with safety rules on it.
3.youHave: a real project on GitHub that rejects unsafe changes

4.plain: Now you write the app itself — and three things go IN the code: sign-in (auth), talking to the database (ORM), and self-reporting (observability: logs, metrics, traces).
4.youHave: an app that authenticates, stores data, and reports its own health
4.next: a fast loop to work in

5.plain: The loop you live in: run the app, see changes instantly, and every save gets auto-checked for mistakes and leaked passwords before it leaves your machine.
5.youHave: a dev loop that catches problems before anyone else sees them
5.next: pack the app into a container

6.plain: A container is a sealed box holding your app and everything it needs, so it runs the same on any computer. This step picks the box and writes its recipe (Dockerfile).
6.youHave: a container image — your app, sealed and portable

7.plain: Before any change joins the main code, robots inspect it: tests, code scans, secret leaks, bad licenses. A PR (pull request — a proposed change) only passes clean.
7.youHave: a gate that blocks bad code automatically

8.plain: When a change is accepted, the real build runs: the container is built, signed (tamper-proof seal), gets an ingredient list (SBOM), and is pushed to the registry (the image warehouse).
8.youHave: a signed, traceable image in the warehouse

9.plain: You never deploy by hand. You write DOWN what should run — in Git. Helm and Kustomize are the two ways to write per-environment settings (dev vs prod).
9.youHave: your desired state written down, reviewable like code
9.next: let the robot make it real

10.plain: A controller (Argo CD) constantly compares what Git says vs what the cluster (the machines that run containers) actually runs — and fixes the difference. One controller, every cluster.
10.youHave: your app running in the cluster, kept in sync automatically

11.plain: Running is not done. Watch four signals — speed, traffic, errors, fullness — and let alerts wake you only when the user experience is truly at risk.
11.youHave: eyes on the service: dashboards, alerts, an error budget

12.plain: Real services talk to others — payments, identity, government systems. Each connection goes through a gateway (a guarded front door) with its own sign-in.
12.youHave: your industry's external connections, mapped and guarded

13.plain: The close of the story: every rule from Scene 1, checked. Met, forced, or gap. Release when the gaps are closed — and you can show an auditor exactly why.
13.youHave: a service you can defend in an audit

14.plain: Software rots quietly. Every week, small updates arrive — libraries, base images. A robot opens the update PRs; you review and merge while they're still small.
14.youHave: a service that stays current in small, safe steps

15.plain: The platform under you moves too — Kubernetes versions retire on a schedule whether you act or not. Upgrade staging first, soak, then prod, on YOUR calendar.
15.youHave: a platform upgraded on your terms, never the provider's deadline

16.plain: Certificates expire. Secrets age. Disks fail. Rotation is automated — but a backup you never restored is a wish, not a backup. Drill the restore.
16.youHave: automated rotation + a restore you have actually proven
16.next: be ready when it breaks anyway

17.plain: Something will break at 2am. The difference between a blip and a disaster is a calm, written path: page → acknowledge → mitigate → learn, blamelessly.
17.youHave: an on-call that follows a runbook, not adrenaline
17.next: keep the whole thing worth running

18.plain: Every quarter, look up: what's reaching end-of-life, what's bleeding money, what should retire. Evolving on purpose is what keeps the platform young.
18.youHave: a stack with no surprise end-of-life and no zombie spend
18.next: back to Scene 14 — the loop continues

nodejs.what: Structured logs + metrics + traces — the app reports itself
Verify the caller's token on every protected request
Frontend: real-user speed numbers sent to your backend

python.code:
import structlog
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, generate_latest
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.fastapi import FastAPIInstrumentor

log = structlog.get_logger()
http_duration = Histogram('http_request_duration_seconds', 'request duration', ['method', 'route', 'status'])

def metrics_text():
    return generate_latest()

# call once at startup: FastAPIInstrumentor.instrument_app(app)

import os, jwt
from jwt import PyJWKClient
from fastapi import HTTPException, Header

_jwks = PyJWKClient(os.environ['OIDC_JWKS_URL'])

def require_user(authorization: str = Header(None)):
    if not authorization:
        raise HTTPException(401, 'missing token')
    token = authorization.removeprefix('Bearer ')
    key = _jwks.get_signing_key_from_jwt(token).key
    return jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=['RS256'],
                      audience=os.environ['OIDC_AUDIENCE'],
                      issuer=os.environ['OIDC_ISSUER'])

java.what: Structured logs + metrics — the app reports itself
Verify the caller's token on every protected request

ruby.code:
require 'semantic_logger'
require 'prometheus/client'

# logs: one JSON line per event
SemanticLogger.add_appender(io: $stdout, formatter: :json)
Rails.logger = SemanticLogger['app']

# metrics: registry scraped at /metrics (mount Prometheus::Middleware::Exporter)
PROM = Prometheus::Client.registry
HTTP_DURATION = PROM.histogram(:http_request_duration_seconds,
  docstring: 'request duration', labels: [:method, :route, :status])

require 'jwt'
require 'net/http'

# Verifies the caller's token against your OIDC JWKS on every protected action.
module Authenticatable
  extend ActiveSupport::Concern
  included { before_action :require_user }

  def require_user
    token = request.headers['Authorization'].to_s.delete_prefix('Bearer ')
    jwks = JSON.parse(Net::HTTP.get(URI(ENV['OIDC_JWKS_URL'])))
    @claims, = JWT.decode(token, nil, true,
      algorithms: ['RS256'], jwks: jwks,
      aud: ENV['OIDC_AUDIENCE'], verify_aud: true,
      iss: ENV['OIDC_ISSUER'], verify_iss: true)
  rescue JWT::DecodeError
    render json: { error: 'unauthorized' }, status: 401
  end
end

php.code:
<?php
namespace App\Providers;

use Illuminate\Support\ServiceProvider;
use Prometheus\CollectorRegistry;
use Prometheus\Storage\InMemory;

// logs: Monolog JSON — set 'formatter' => JsonFormatter::class in config/logging.php
// metrics: promphp/prometheus_client_php exposed at GET /metrics
class ObservabilityProvider extends ServiceProvider
{
    public function register(): void
    {
        $this->app->singleton(CollectorRegistry::class,
            fn () => new CollectorRegistry(new InMemory()));
    }
}

<?php
namespace App\Http\Middleware;

use Closure;
use Firebase\JWT\JWT;
use Firebase\JWT\JWK;

// Verifies the bearer token against your OIDC JWKS on every protected route.
class VerifyJwt
{
    public function handle($request, Closure $next)
    {
        $token = str_replace('Bearer ', '', $request->header('Authorization', ''));
        $jwks = json_decode(file_get_contents(env('OIDC_JWKS_URL')), true);
        try {
            $claims = JWT::decode($token, JWK::parseKeySet($jwks));
            $request->attributes->set('claims', $claims);
        } catch (\Throwable $e) {
            return response()->json(['error' => 'unauthorized'], 401);
        }
        return $next($request);
    }
}

elixir.code:
defmodule App.Observability do
  # logs: LoggerJSON for one JSON line per event
  # metrics: Telemetry + TelemetryMetricsPrometheus exposes GET /metrics
  def metrics do
    [
      Telemetry.Metrics.distribution("http.request.duration.seconds",
        event_name: [:phoenix, :endpoint, :stop],
        measurement: :duration, unit: {:native, :second},
        tags: [:method, :route, :status])
    ]
  end
end
# config: config :logger, :default_handler,
#   formatter: {LoggerJSON.Formatters.Basic, []}

defmodule AppWeb.Plugs.RequireUser do
  # Verifies the bearer token against your OIDC issuer using Joken.
  import Plug.Conn

  def init(opts), do: opts

  def call(conn, _opts) do
    with ["Bearer " <> token] <- get_req_header(conn, "authorization"),
         {:ok, claims} <- App.Token.verify_and_validate(token) do
      assign(conn, :claims, claims)
    else
      _ -> conn |> send_resp(401, "unauthorized") |> halt()
    end
  end
end

commands.label: Create the cluster
commands.cmd: eksctl create cluster --name prod --region ca-central-1 --nodes 3 --node-type m6i.large --with-oidc
commands.expect: ~15 min; ends with 'EKS cluster "prod" is ready'

commands.label: Point kubectl at it
commands.expect: Added new context to kubeconfig

commands.label: Create the cluster (autopilot)
commands.cmd: gcloud container clusters create-auto prod --region northamerica-northeast1
commands.expect: ~7 min; kubeconfig entry generated

commands.label: Point kubectl at it
commands.cmd: gcloud container clusters get-credentials prod --region northamerica-northeast1
commands.expect: kubeconfig entry generated for prod

commands.label: Create the cluster
commands.expect: ~10 min; JSON description of the cluster

commands.label: Point kubectl at it
commands.expect: Merged "prod" as current context

commands.label: Managed on Azure (ARO)
commands.cmd: az aro create -g prod-rg -n prod --vnet aro-vnet --master-subnet master --worker-subnet worker
commands.expect: ~35 min; cluster JSON with consoleProfile URL

commands.label: Self-managed (IPI)
commands.expect: ~40 min; kubeadmin password printed at the end

1.skipWhen: Never. Even an internal tool has a user and rules.
1.threat: Building to the wrong rules. Retrofitting compliance after launch costs 10–100× and can mean fines.

rejected.why: Scenes 4, 6, 7 and 10 are SHAPED by these rules — later means rebuilding them.

1.doneWhen: Your industry chip shows in the stack bar, and the rulebook lists your regimes.

breaks.fix: Re-run Scenes 4–13 against the new rulebook; the audit shows exactly what changes.

2.who: The tech lead, with the team
2.skipWhen: Never — even ‘no framework’ is a framework choice with consequences.
2.threat: Hype-driven choice. A dead-end framework means a full rewrite in 18 months.

rejected.why: You re-implement routing, auth and security alone — and fail the audits the framework community already passed.

2.doneWhen: The scaffold command for your pick runs successfully in Scene 3.

breaks.fix: Scene 18 catches it at the quarterly EOL review; plan the migration there.

3.skipWhen: Never. The lockfile and branch protection born here protect every later scene.
3.threat: Typosquatted packages — one mistyped scaffold name installs an attacker's code (supply-chain attack).

rejected.path: Create folders and files by hand
rejected.why: Hand-rolled projects drift: no lockfile, no hygiene, no two alike.

3.doneWhen: The repo exists on GitHub, main is protected, and the lockfile is committed.

breaks.fix: Your Node is too old — install 22 LTS, re-run.
That's branch protection working — open a PR instead.

prereqs: Your identity provider's issuer URL + audience (or dev defaults)
4.skipWhen: Auth may be ‘none’ ONLY for an internal prototype with zero personal data — and the audit will say so.
4.threat: Broken authentication — the OWASP #1. Account takeover, and breaches you can't even see without audit logs.

rejected.why: OIDC is a verified protocol; home-made auth fails real attacks and real audits.

4.doneWhen: A login round-trips locally and /metrics returns live numbers.

breaks.fix: JWKS URL or issuer env is wrong — print the env, compare with your IdP.
The /metrics route isn't mounted — wire metricsText() to a route.

4.time: Half a day to two days
4.cost: Libraries free; managed IdPs (Auth0/Entra) bill per user

5.skipWhen: Hooks: never — they cost seconds. Heavy scans stay in CI, not here.
5.threat: A committed secret. Leaked keys are scraped and abused within minutes of reaching GitHub.

rejected.why: By the time CI sees a pushed secret, it is already public history.

5.doneWhen: A commit containing a fake secret is BLOCKED on your machine.

breaks.fix: Scope them to changed files; heavy checks belong in Scene 7.
Add a scoped allowlist entry in the gitleaks config — never disable the hook.

6.skipWhen: Only for serverless-zip deploys with no containers anywhere — rare on this platform.
6.threat: A bloated, vulnerable base image — every extra package is attack surface you now own.

rejected.path: One fat ubuntu image for everything
rejected.why: 10× the size, hundreds of CVE findings, slow pulls — for zero benefit.

6.doneWhen: docker run answers on /health from the sealed container.

breaks.symptom: Works locally, dies in the container
breaks.fix: Port or env var missing — compare ENV/EXPOSE with your local run.
glibc-vs-musl issue — switch to slim or distroless.

7.who: Platform sets the gates; every dev lives under them
7.skipWhen: A solo throwaway may drop DAST. Secrets and dependency scans: never.
7.threat: One malicious or careless PR reaching main unreviewed — that's how most real breaches start.

rejected.why: One bad merge is a prod incident with no evidence trail for the audit.

7.doneWhen: A test PR with a seeded fake secret fails the gate, red.

breaks.fix: Pin the tool versions; rerun; never mark the gate optional.
Cache dependencies; run gates in parallel.

7.cost: GitHub-hosted minutes are free for public repos; private repos bill past the free tier

prereqs: Registry account + role for your pick (see the registries below)
8.skipWhen: Never for anything that deploys. Unsigned images are unverifiable.
8.threat: Tampering between build and deploy. Signing + SBOM close the supply-chain gap.

rejected.why: Untraceable artifacts: no provenance, no signature, no audit answer to ‘where did this image come from?’

8.doneWhen: cosign verify (or notation verify) passes against the pushed digest.

breaks.fix: Login/role wrong — re-run the registry's login command; check the OIDC role.
The workflow needs permissions: id-token: write.

8.cost: Registry storage — free tiers exist (GHCR free for public)

9.skipWhen: A single-env hobby project can use raw manifests — until the second environment appears.
9.threat: Snowflake environments — config drift nobody can see or reproduce.

rejected.path: kubectl edit on the live cluster
rejected.why: Untracked changes vanish on the next sync and skip every review.

9.doneWhen: kustomize build renders cleanly for every environment.

breaks.fix: diff the overlays — the drift is visible in Git, fix it there.
helm lint; check values types against the chart.

10.who: Platform team owns it; developers consume it
prereqs: A cluster (or create it right here)
10.skipWhen: Only when there is no cluster at all (pure serverless).
10.threat: Manual deploys: nobody knows who changed what; drift accumulates silently.

rejected.path: CI runs kubectl apply with cluster credentials
rejected.why: Cluster creds in CI = huge blast radius, and nothing reconciles drift afterwards.

10.doneWhen: The Argo app reports Synced + Healthy.

breaks.fix: Registry pull secret missing — wire it per the registry setup in Scene 8.
An immutable field or hook loops — check argocd app diff.

10.cost: The cluster is the real cost — roughly $150–300+/month for 3 small nodes

11.when: The day the service goes live
prereqs: Prometheus stack installed (it's in the Scene 10 components)
11.skipWhen: Alerts: never. Full tracing can wait at very small scale — say so in the audit.
11.threat: Silent failure — users discover the outage before you do.

rejected.path: Grep the logs when someone complains
rejected.why: No SLO, no paging, no early warning — and complaints arrive after the damage.

11.doneWhen: A test alert fires and reaches your channel/phone.

breaks.fix: Switch to burn-rate alerts (the SLO starter above) — page on budget burn, not every blip.
Check the scrape annotations/ServiceMonitor on the deployment.

11.cost: OSS stack free; SaaS observability bills per GB ingested

12.who: App dev + the partner's integration team
12.when: When the product needs the outside world
prereqs: A sandbox environment from the partner
12.skipWhen: Only if the service truly talks to nothing external — rare and usually temporary.
12.threat: A third-party breach reaching you through over-scoped, never-rotated credentials.

rejected.path: Direct point-to-point calls from app code
rejected.why: No throttle, no audit, no single place to rotate or cut access.

12.doneWhen: A sandbox call round-trips through the gateway with scoped credentials.

breaks.fix: A cert expired — rotate it; Scene 16 automates this.
Honour the partner's limits — add backoff at the gateway.

12.time: Days per integration — the partner sets the pace

13.skipWhen: Never for a regulated industry. For a hobby, it's still 30 free minutes of truth.
13.threat: Shipping with silent gaps — found later by an auditor, or worse, by a breach.

rejected.why: Evidence captured while building is cheap; reconstructing it later costs 10×.

13.doneWhen: Zero red gaps — or every gap has an owner and a date.

breaks.symptom: A gap can't be closed now
breaks.fix: Document the compensating control + owner + date; that line IS audit language.

14.who: The whole team; a robot opens the PRs
prereqs: Tests you trust (they are the safety net)
14.skipWhen: Never — rot compounds. Pausing for a release week is fine; stopping is not.
14.threat: A known-CVE dependency sitting unpatched — the Equifax pattern.

rejected.why: Ninety small, safe merges beat one risky big-bang every time.

14.doneWhen: This week's update PRs are merged or consciously triaged.

breaks.symptom: An update PR breaks the build
breaks.fix: That's the system working — read the changelog, adapt, or pin with a dated TODO.
Let the bot rebase: re-run it; merge oldest first.

15.who: Platform team, in a maintenance window
15.when: Quarterly, or when the provider announces EOL
15.skipWhen: Never — Kubernetes versions retire on the provider's calendar whether you act or not.
15.threat: A forced emergency upgrade at end-of-life, with zero test time.

rejected.why: Patches stop arriving, and managed providers force-upgrade control planes anyway.

15.doneWhen: Staging upgraded + one week soak, then prod by the same path.

breaks.fix: Run pluto detect-files BEFORE upgrading; fix manifests first.
Upgrade order: control plane → nodes → addons; check each addon's matrix.

16.threat: An expired certificate taking prod down — or a backup that turns out unrestorable mid-disaster.

rejected.path: Manual annual renewals on a calendar reminder

16.doneWhen: A restore DRILL succeeded into a scratch namespace this quarter.

breaks.fix: Alert on the backup job itself — a missing backup is a page, not a surprise.

breaks.symptom: Secret rotated but pods use the old one
breaks.fix: Add a reloader so deployments restart on secret change.

16.time: A day to set up; 2-hour drill each quarter

17.when: Before the first real incident, not after
17.skipWhen: Never in production. The 2am page is a when, not an if.
17.threat: Chaos during an incident multiplies the damage — and for Financial Services, the OSFI 24-hour reporting clock is already running.

rejected.why: Untracked mid-incident changes are how incident #1 births incident #2.

17.doneWhen: One game-day completed: page → acknowledge → mitigate → blameless postmortem.

breaks.symptom: An alert fires with no runbook
breaks.fix: Mitigate, then write the runbook the same day — that alert now has an owner.
The postmortem actions weren't done — they go on the board with owners, now.

17.time: A day for the process + quarterly game-days

prereqs: The version pins this studio tracks
A cost dashboard from your cloud
18.skipWhen: Never past the first year — entropy and spend only grow.
18.threat: Running end-of-life software you can no longer patch — and zombie services bleeding money.

rejected.why: Every running thing is attack surface plus spend; sunset is a feature.

18.doneWhen: This quarter's review done: EOL list empty or planned; top-3 cost actions taken.

breaks.symptom: Something hits EOL with no upgrade path
breaks.fix: Isolate it (network policy), plan the replacement, set the date.
Tag everything; sweep untagged monthly.

auto: Tests run on every update PR
Base images rebuilt on schedule (Scene 8 workflow)

commands.label: See this week's update PRs
commands.expect: the open update PRs, oldest first

commands.label: Check what's outdated right now
commands.cmd: npm outdated || pip list --outdated || go list -u -m all
commands.expect: current vs latest versions, per package

auto: Provider patches the control plane within a minor
Argo re-syncs apps after node rotation

commands.label: Find removed APIs before upgrading
commands.expect: a table of deprecated/removed APIs (empty = safe)

commands.label: Upgrade the cluster (your provider)
commands.cmd: eksctl upgrade cluster --name prod --approve   # or: gcloud container clusters upgrade / az aks upgrade
commands.expect: control plane on the next minor

commands.label: Then nodes, then addons
commands.expect: nodes rotated to the new version

auto: you add a reloader for pods

commands.label: Check certificate health
commands.expect: READY True on every cert

commands.label: Take a backup now
commands.expect: Backup request "pre-drill" submitted

commands.label: THE DRILL — restore into scratch
commands.expect: the app comes up in the drill namespace

sev: page now, all hands, 24h regulator clock may start
runbook: Open the linked runbook — follow it, don't improvise.
Mitigate FIRST (rollback/scale/failover). Root cause comes later.
Note times + actions in the incident channel as you go.
Declare resolved; schedule the blameless postmortem within 48h.
Postmortem: timeline, causes, action items WITH owners.

commands.label: Roll back fast (GitOps way)
commands.cmd: git revert HEAD && git push   # Argo syncs the rollback
commands.expect: previous version live within the sync interval

commands.label: Or pause sync and roll back direct
commands.cmd: argocd app set APP --sync-policy none && kubectl rollout undo deploy/APP -n prod
commands.expect: deployment rolled back; re-enable sync after

auto: This studio pins every version (148 tracked)
you review the EOL list quarterly
you act on the top 3 costs

commands.label: Check a component's end-of-life
commands.cmd: open https://endoflife.date/kubernetes   # one page per major product
commands.expect: dates — plan upgrades BEFORE the red ones

commands.label: Find untagged (orphan) cloud resources
commands.expect: resources nobody owns — candidates to sweep

maintCalendar: Merge/triage update PRs (S14) · scan alert noise (S11)
Base-image rebuild lands (S8 auto) · sweep orphan resources (S18)
K8s upgrade (S15) · restore DRILL (S16) · game-day (S17) · EOL + cost review (S18) · access review (rulebook)
Pen-test (PCI/OSFI rulebooks) · full DR failover · postmortem-actions audit

jurisdictions.pipeda: Federal Canada — private-sector orgs in every province without their own substantially-similar law
jurisdictions.phipa: Ontario only — personal health information (custodians + their vendors)
jurisdictions.pipa-ab: Alberta only — private sector (replaces PIPEDA inside Alberta)
jurisdictions.pipa-bc: British Columbia only — private sector (replaces PIPEDA inside BC)
jurisdictions.law25-quebec: Québec only — any business handling Québec residents' data, wherever you are
jurisdictions.osfi-b13: Federal Canada — banks, insurers and federally-regulated financial institutions
jurisdictions.osfi-b10: Federal Canada — third-party risk for the same institutions
jurisdictions.fintrac-pcmltfa: Federal Canada — anyone moving money: banks, MSBs, real estate, casinos
jurisdictions.pci-dss-4: Global — contractual: applies the moment you touch payment cards, any country
jurisdictions.hipaa: United States — only if you handle US patients' health data
jurisdictions.soc1: Global — voluntary attestation your enterprise customers ask for
jurisdictions.soc2-typeii: Global — voluntary attestation; the default ask in B2B sales
jurisdictions.iso27001: Global — voluntary certification; the international security baseline
jurisdictions.fedramp-mod: United States — selling cloud to US federal agencies
jurisdictions.fedramp-high: United States — high-impact US federal workloads
jurisdictions.fips-140-3: US/Canada governments — validated crypto modules; Canada recognizes via CCCS
jurisdictions.cmmc-l2: United States — US defense supply chain contracts
jurisdictions.nerc-cip: US + Canadian interconnected grid — bulk electric system operators
jurisdictions.gdpr: European Union — applies if you serve EU residents, wherever you are
jurisdictions.ccpa: California — applies at revenue/volume thresholds serving Californians
jurisdictions.ffiec: United States — US banking regulators' IT handbook
jurisdictions.soci: Australia (+ Canadian critical-infra equivalents) — critical infrastructure duties
jurisdictions.dora: European Union — financial entities + their ICT providers serving the EU

steps.title: Who are you building for?
steps.why: This single choice writes your rulebook: which laws apply, where your data must live, how strong sign-in must be. Everything after reads it.
steps.consequence: Writes the rulebook every later decision must obey.

steps.title: Pick your framework
steps.why: The framework is your foundation — it sets your language, your hiring pool, and what 'idiomatic' means for the next five years.
steps.consequence: Sets the language — package manager, ORM and Dockerfile all follow it.

steps.title: Pick your package manager
steps.why: It creates the lockfile — the frozen list of exact versions that makes every build identical, on your laptop and in CI.
steps.consequence: Sets the install command in CI and the Dockerfile's dependency layer.

steps.title: How will people sign in?
steps.why: Broken authentication is the #1 web risk. Your industry's rulebook may already demand MFA (a second proof beyond the password).
steps.consequence: Becomes middleware in your app and an AUTH build-arg in CI.

steps.title: How will the app talk to the database?
steps.why: An ORM maps code objects to database rows. The right one gives type-safety — wrong queries caught before they run.
steps.consequence: Shapes your data layer and migrations from day one.

steps.title: How will the service report its own health?
steps.why: Logs, metrics, traces — the three signals. Regulated industries require audit-grade logging; everyone needs to see failures before users do.
steps.consequence: Wired into the app now; Scene 11's dashboards read it later.

steps.title: Pick the container base
steps.why: The base image is the floor your app stands on. Smaller floor, fewer holes. Some rulebooks force FIPS-validated crypto floors.
steps.consequence: Rewrites the FROM line of your generated Dockerfile — literally.

steps.title: Where will your images live?
steps.why: The registry is the warehouse for your sealed app. Same-cloud pulls are faster; OIDC means no stored passwords; residency may be a rule.
steps.consequence: Rewrites your CI workflow's push/login steps to that registry.

steps.title: How will you prove the image is yours?
steps.why: Signing seals the image; the SBOM lists its ingredients. Together they answer the auditor's 'where did this come from?'
steps.consequence: Sets the signing + SBOM steps in your main workflow.

steps.title: Where will it run?
steps.why: The cluster is the fleet of machines running your containers. Your rulebook may prefer private, Canadian-region, or OpenShift.
steps.consequence: Sets the provisioning commands and where Argo CD deploys.

frontend-web.name: A web app
frontend-web.plain: What users see and click in a browser.

mobile.name: A mobile app
mobile.plain: Installs on phones — App Store and Play Store.

backend.name: A backend service
backend.plain: The API and logic behind apps — no screen of its own.

protocol.name: A real-time / protocol service
protocol.plain: Live connections and typed APIs — gRPC, GraphQL, WebSocket.

catPlain.c1: Pages render on the server first — fast first paint and SEO; the browser takes over after.
catPlain.c2: The browser builds everything — instant app feel after load; SEO needs extra work.
catPlain.c3: Pages are pre-built at publish time — unbeatably fast for content that rarely changes.
catPlain.c4: Mostly static pages with small interactive islands — content sites that need a little app.
catPlain.c5: No hydration replay at all — the page resumes where the server left off; fastest interactivity.
catPlain.c6: Renders at the CDN edge, close to every user — lowest latency worldwide.
catPlain.c7: The server streams HTML as it's ready — users see content before the page finishes.
catPlain.c8: Many teams ship one site as independent pieces — for large orgs, not small apps.
catPlain.c9: One JavaScript codebase, real iOS + Android apps — the common cross-platform path.
catPlain.c10: One codebase, not JavaScript (Dart/Kotlin/C#) — native-feel performance.
catPlain.c11: Swift, Apple's tools only — the deepest iOS experience.
catPlain.c12: Kotlin, Google's tools only — the deepest Android experience.
catPlain.c13: A website that installs like an app — no app store, one codebase, some limits.
catPlain.c14: JavaScript/TypeScript on the server — your web team's language, end to end.
catPlain.c15: Python — fast to write, the data/AI ecosystem next door.
catPlain.c16: Go — small fast binaries, effortless concurrency; the cloud-native default.
catPlain.c17: Java — the enterprise workhorse: mature, staffed, audited everywhere.
catPlain.c18: Kotlin on the JVM — Java's ecosystem with a modern language.
catPlain.c19: .NET / C# — Microsoft's stack; first-class on Azure and in enterprises.
catPlain.c20: Rust — maximum speed and safety; steeper learning curve, no garbage collector.
catPlain.c21: Elixir — millions of cheap live connections; built for always-on systems.
catPlain.c22: Ruby — developer happiness and speed-to-market; Rails carries whole products.
catPlain.c23: PHP — runs most of the web; modern frameworks are far from the old jokes.
catPlain.c24: Swift on the server — share code with your iOS app.
catPlain.c25: Scala — functional power on the JVM; data-heavy systems.
catPlain.c26: Clojure — a Lisp on the JVM; small expert teams, very stable cores.
catPlain.c27: C++ — when every microsecond is money: trading, games, embedded.
catPlain.c28: gRPC — typed, binary, fast service-to-service calls; the microservices standard.
catPlain.c29: GraphQL — clients ask for exactly the data they need; one endpoint, typed schema.
catPlain.c30: WebSocket — a connection that stays open both ways; chat, live dashboards, games.

industryPlain.financial-services: Banks, lenders, money apps — the heaviest money-movement rules in Canada.
industryPlain.healthcare-and-life-sciences: Hospitals, clinics, health apps — patient data has its own provincial laws.
industryPlain.government-and-public-sector: Serving government — citizen data must stay sovereign and classified-safe.
industryPlain.defense-and-intelligence: Defence work — classified data, security clearances, export controls.
industryPlain.energy-and-environment: Power, pipelines, utilities — systems that move physical things safely.
industryPlain.technology: Software companies and SaaS — the lightest rulebook; B2B buyers ask for SOC 2.
industryPlain.telecommunications: Networks and carriers — critical infrastructure with privacy duties.
industryPlain.retail-and-commerce: Stores and e-commerce — the moment you take cards, card rules apply.
industryPlain.manufacturing-and-industrial: Factories and industrial systems — uptime and supply-chain security.
industryPlain.media-and-entertainment: Content, streaming, games-adjacent — piracy and audience privacy.
industryPlain.education: Schools and ed-tech — students' records are protected data.
industryPlain.transportation-and-logistics: Moving people and goods — control systems and critical infrastructure.
industryPlain.real-estate-and-construction: Property and building — wire-fraud is the classic attack here.
industryPlain.agriculture: Farms and food chains — traceability rules for recalls.
industryPlain.legal-and-professional-services: Law and consulting firms — client confidentiality above all.
industryPlain.non-profit-and-social: Charities and NGOs — donor trust and donation card data.

auth-oidc: The standard “Sign in with…” — proves WHO the user is.
auth-oauth2-mfa: Sign-in plus a second proof — a phone prompt or security key.
auth-oauth2: Lets an app act for a user with limited permissions.
auth-jwt: A signed pass-card your API checks on every request.
auth-mtls: Machines prove themselves to EACH OTHER with certificates.
auth-apikey: A long password for another PROGRAM, not a person.
auth-saml: Big-company single sign-on — the older enterprise standard.
auth-none: No sign-in at all. Only for throwaway demos with no real data.
auth-all: The full stack: user sign-in + MFA + machine certificates. What regulated industries actually run.

observability-otel: One standard way to emit logs, metrics and traces — works with every backend.
observability-prometheus: Counts and timings, scraped and graphed — the metrics standard.
observability-grafana-stack: Grafana's full open-source set: logs + traces + metrics dashboards.
observability-elastic: Search-everything logs — also the audit/SIEM choice for regulated shops.
observability-datadog: Everything as a paid service — fastest to start, bills per GB.
observability-none: Flying blind. The service can't tell you it's failing.

orm-prisma: Define your data once, get type-safe queries — the friendly default.
orm-typeorm: Classic decorator-style models — mature, lots of examples.
orm-drizzle: SQL-first and featherweight — you stay close to the database.
orm-sqlalchemy: Python's standard for talking to databases — powerful and everywhere.
orm-tortoise: Async-first Python models — pairs naturally with FastAPI.
orm-gorm: Go's most-used database layer — conventions over ceremony.
orm-sqlx: No magic: you write SQL, it checks it at compile time.
orm-none: Raw SQL by hand — full control, all responsibility.

alpine: Tiny with a shell — easy to debug, musl quirks exist.
slim: Small Debian — maximum compatibility, no surprises.
distroless: No shell, no package manager — almost nothing to attack.
fips: Government-validated crypto build — what FIPS rulebooks demand.
ubi-micro: Red Hat's minimal base — enterprise support paths.
scratch: Literally empty — for static Go/Rust binaries only.
wolfi: Chainguard's zero-CVE-target distro — fresh and minimal.

eks: Kubernetes run by AWS — the most common choice.
gke: Kubernetes run by Google — the smoothest autopilot.
aks: Kubernetes run by Microsoft — natural for Azure shops.
openshift: Red Hat's hardened Kubernetes — banks and governments pick it.

npm: The installer that comes with Node — nothing to set up.
pnpm: Faster installs that save disk by storing each package once.
yarn: A fast installer with a rich plugin system.
bun: An all-in-one tool that installs and runs JavaScript very fast.
poetry: Python installer that also manages your project and lockfile.
uv: A very fast Python installer written in Rust.
gomod: Go's built-in dependency system — no extra tool needed.
maven: The long-standing Java build and dependency tool.
gradle: A flexible build tool for Java, Kotlin and Android.
tsc: The official TypeScript compiler — turns typed code into JavaScript.
esbuild: An extremely fast bundler written in Go.
swc: A fast Rust-based compiler, a drop-in for Babel.
vite: A modern dev server and bundler with instant startup.
turbopack: Next.js's Rust bundler, built for very large apps.

ghcr: GitHub's image store — zero setup with GitHub Actions.
ecr: Amazon's image store — the natural pick on AWS.
harbor: An image store you host yourself, with built-in scanning.
quay: Red Hat's image store, with built-in security scanning.
dockerhub: The original public image store — easy, but rate-limited.
jfrog: An enterprise artifact store for many package types.
nexus: A self-hosted artifact store for many package types.

cosign: Seals your image so tampering shows — no keys to manage.
notation: Signs images using your own certificate authority.

syft: Lists every ingredient inside your image — the standard tool.
cdxgen: Builds the ingredient list in the CycloneDX format.
trivy: Scans for vulnerabilities and can also produce the list.

ruleLine.auth: Your rulebook for {IND}: people must sign in with a second proof (MFA), and services must verify each other. One pick below covers all of it — that's the recommendation.
ruleLine.obs: Your rulebook for {IND} demands audit-grade logging. The recommendation below satisfies it.
ruleLine.runtime: Your rulebook for {IND} requires validated-crypto (FIPS) bases. The recommendation below is one.
ruleLine.cluster: Your rulebook for {IND} prefers private, regulated-ready clusters. The recommendation below fits.

primer.what: A deployment pipeline is the automated path your code takes from your laptop to running in front of real users.
primer.why: Doing those steps by hand is slow and easy to get wrong. The pipeline does them the same way every time, so a mistake can't quietly ship.
steps: Who is this for? A bank and a blog follow different laws. That choice sets every rule below.
Pick a framework, write your code, add sign-in and a database.
Package the app into a container — a sealed box that runs the same everywhere.
Before code is allowed in, robots run tests and scan for security holes.
The approved code is built, cryptographically signed, and saved to a registry.
You declare what should run in Git. A controller makes the cluster match.
Track speed, traffic, errors. Get paged before users notice.
Update weekly, patch the platform, rotate secrets, and have a 2am plan.
primer.promise: This tool walks all of that, one decision at a time, in plain words. At the end you have real files you can commit.
primer.jargonNote: Every underlined term has a plain-English definition — tap it. Nothing here assumes you already know the words.

auth-mtls.note: Built for service-to-service calls, not human sign-in. Pick it backend-to-backend, not for a login page.

auth-apikey.note: Identifies an app, not a person. Use for machine callers, not human login.

auth-none.note: No sign-in at all. Fine for public read-only pages; never where user data or a regime is involved.

orm-none.note: No database layer. Fine for stateless APIs; pick a real ORM once you store data.

observability-none.note: No monitoring. Acceptable for a throwaway demo only; never for anything users depend on.

scratch.note: Empty base — no shell, no libc. Only for static Go/Rust binaries; hard to debug for beginners.
